questions.text: When the Castellan asks Cellini if he would attempt to fly, how does Cellini respond?
questions.held_out_passage: I answered that it had always been my ambition to do those things which offer the greatest difficulties to men, and that I had done them; as to flying, the God of Nature had gifted me with a body well suited for running and leaping far beyond the common average, and that with the talents I possessed for manual art I felt sure I had the courage to try flying.

questions.text: When the Castellan orders Cellini locked up, how does Cellini respond before being confined?
questions.held_out_passage: I told him before all his servants: 'Lock me well up, and keep good watch on me; for I shall certainly contrive to escape.'

questions.text: What is Cellini's immediate action upon being locked in his prison?
questions.held_out_passage: No sooner had I been locked in, than I went about exploring my prison; and when I thought I had discovered how to get out of it, I pondered the means of descending from the lofty keep

questions.text: What materials does Cellini use to prepare for his escape attempt?
questions.held_out_passage: I took those new sheets of mine, which, as I have said already, I had cut in strips and sewn together

questions.text: How does Cellini obtain the pincers he needs for his escape?
questions.held_out_passage: I looked out a pair of pincers which I had abstracted from a Savoyard belonging to the guard of the castle. This man superintended the casks and cisterns; he also amused himself with carpentering. Now he possessed several pairs of pincers, among which was one both big and heavy. I then, thinking it would suit my purpose, took it and hid it in my straw mattress.

questions.text: What is Cellini's relationship with the Castellan based on the training text's description of his character?
questions.held_out_passage: since they had noticed that he derived much pleasure from my conversation, they were always fetching me to keep him company

questions.text: When the Castellan becomes fixated on the bat idea, what does he do?
questions.held_out_passage: No sooner had the poor man heard the name bat, which recalled the humour he was suffering under, than he cried out at the top of his voice: 'He says true-he says true; the bat's the thing-the bat's the thing!'

questions.held_out_passage: The door was double, and the clinching of the nails could not be seen; so that when I attempted to draw one out, I met with the greatest trouble; in the end, however, I succeeded.

questions.text: When Cellini successfully removes the first nail from his door hinges, what does he do to conceal the evidence?

questions.text: How does Cellini respond to the guard Giovanni's offensive remarks and close inspections?
questions.held_out_passage: I used to say: 'Keep a good watch over me, for I am resolved by all means to escape.' These words bred a great enmity between him and me, so that I was obliged to use precautions to conceal my tools.

questions.text: Does Cellini hide his escape tools or leave them visible in his cell?

questions.text: What does Cellini do each morning to maintain his appearance while planning his escape?
questions.held_out_passage: When day broke, I used immediately to sweep my room out; and though I am by nature a lover of cleanliness, at that time I kept myself unusually spick and span.
